title: Vanishing Theorems in Algebraic Geometry
speaker: Bhargav Bhatt
affiliation: Fernholz Joint Professor; IAS School of Mathematics
abstract: Vanishing theorems in complex algebraic geometry, such as the Kodaira vanishing theorem, play an important role in understanding the structure of complex algebraic varieties. Ultimately, they rely on critical input from Hodge theory. After recalling some aspects of this story, I will discuss analogous results in mixed characteristic as well as applications; the key input now comes from $p$-adic Hodge theory.
tags: Conference on 100 Years of Noetherian Rings

proof_stages.label: Classical coherent vanishing baseline
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up Serre and Kodaira vanishing over the complex numbers, including the Serre-dual canonical-bundle form of Kodaira vanishing and the kinds of geometric consequences one wants to preserve.

proof_stages.label: Hodge-theoretic mechanism over C
proof_stages.summary: Kodaira vanishing is related by Hodge theory to a topological statement: high-degree cohomology of a smooth affine complex variety vanishes because it has the homotopy type of a CW complex of real dimension equal to its complex dimension.

proof_stages.label: Failure over other bases and finite-cover philosophy
proof_stages.summary: Literal Kodaira vanishing is recalled to fail in positive and mixed characteristic, motivating the replacement principle that unwanted cohomology classes should be killed after passing to finite covers.

proof_stages.label: Mixed-characteristic global finite-cover vanishing
proof_stages.summary: Theorem A asserts that for a projective scheme over Z_p with ample line bundle L, a finite cover kills the p-torsion parts of cohomology with coefficients L^k for k=-1,0,1, and this up-to-cover vanishing supplies input for mixed-characteristic MMP results.

proof_stages.label: Cone dictionary and local vanishing theorem
proof_stages.summary: Passing from a projective variety to its affine cone translates the global finite-cover story into local algebra, yielding Theorem B that R^+/p is Cohen--Macaulay, equivalently that finite extensions kill bad relations among parameters.

proof_stages.label: p-adic Riemann--Hilbert replacement for Hodge theory
proof_stages.summary: A mod-p Riemann--Hilbert functor from constructible F_p-sheaves on the geometric generic fiber to modules on the special fiber is introduced as the mixed-characteristic substitute for Hodge theory, with perverse sheaves mapping to almost Cohen--Macaulay complexes.

proof_stages.label: Universal finite-cover input and almost Cohen--Macaulayness
proof_stages.summary: The object R^+/p is identified with the Riemann--Hilbert image of a perverse sheaf, supported by the lemma that the shifted pushforward from the inverse limit of finite covers is perverse, giving the almost Cohen--Macaulay version of Theorem B.

proof_stages.label: Prismatic upgrade to honest vanishing
proof_stages.summary: Prismatic cohomology, especially its Frobenius action, is used to convert the almost vanishing obtained from p-adic Riemann--Hilbert into genuine Cohen--Macaulay/local vanishing.

examples.name: Meromorphic functions with one bounded pole on a curve
examples.note: A compact curve/Riemann surface with a marked point illustrates how Serre vanishing plus Riemann--Roch turns a cohomological vanishing into the concrete dimension formula n plus a constant for functions with pole order at most n.
examples.engagement_reason: He asks for and identifies the dimension growth of the bounded-pole meromorphic functions, explaining the local expectation and the n-plus-constant conclusion via vanishing/Riemann–Roch.

passes.reason: ordinary Riemann--Roch/Serre-vanishing illustration for a divisor supported at one point on a curve
The space of meromorphic functions with a bounded pole at one point is a standard Riemann--Roch/Serre-vanishing illustration, not a structurally special or adversarial object.
The pole divisor nP has collapsed one-point support and is used only as a Serre/Riemann--Roch illustration.
An ordinary Riemann--Roch/Serre-vanishing application to the divisor n·x, used to compute growth rather than refute anything.
An ordinary pole-bounded linear series on a curve is used as a positive illustration of Serre/Riemann--Roch consequences.
An ordinary Riemann--Roch linear series with prescribed pole divisor is used as a positive consequence of vanishing.
Used as a standard Riemann--Roch/Serre-vanishing illustration for a curve, not to refute a claim.
Used as a standard Riemann--Roch/Serre-vanishing consequence for an ordinary linear series on a curve.
A standard linear series of meromorphic functions with prescribed pole divisor is used as an illustrative consequence of Serre vanishing and Riemann--Roch.
Used as a positive Riemann--Roch/Serre-vanishing illustration with an ordinary one-point linear series on a curve.
The allowed polar divisor is collapsed to one point, but it is used as a positive Riemann--Roch/vanishing application.
Used as a standard positive application of Serre vanishing/Riemann--Roch, and the one-point pole linear series is not structurally sporadic or extremal.

examples.name: Lifting canonical sections from a hyperplane section
examples.note: For a smooth hypersurface cut out by an ample line bundle, the restriction/residue exact sequence shows concretely how Kodaira vanishing makes sections on the hypersurface lift to sections upstairs.
examples.engagement_reason: He sets up the hyperplane section, writes the exact sequence for canonical bundles, and chases cohomology to prove the restriction map on sections is surjective.

passes.reason: standard hyperplane-section restriction used positively to lift canonical sections via Kodaira vanishing
The hyperplane-section lifting map is used as a typical positive application of Kodaira vanishing for induction on dimension.
A smooth hyperplane section with the canonical restriction map is a standard projective-geometric situation used positively for induction.
A standard smooth hyperplane-section restriction exact sequence is used positively to lift canonical sections.
A smooth hyperplane section is a standard divisor used to demonstrate a lifting application of Kodaira vanishing.
The hyperplane-section restriction/lifting map is a standard inductive application of Kodaira vanishing.
A hyperplane-section lifting argument is presented as a typical application of Kodaira vanishing.
Used positively to illustrate Kodaira vanishing via surjective restriction from a variety to a smooth hyperplane section.
A smooth hyperplane section is a typical inductive setup used to illustrate Kodaira-vanishing surjectivity of restriction maps.
Used as a standard positive application of Kodaira vanishing to a smooth hyperplane section.
A smooth hyperplane section is an ordinary inductive slice used to lift canonical sections via Kodaira vanishing.
Used as a positive application of Kodaira vanishing to get surjectivity of restriction, with a smooth hyperplane section an ordinary ample divisor.

examples.name: Characteristic-p curve and killing H^1(O_X) by Frobenius and finite étale covers
examples.note: The decomposition of H^1(O_X) into a Frobenius-nilpotent part and an étale/topological part displays the mechanism behind killing classes after finite covers and explains why purely separable covers are insufficient, especially for supersingular curves.
examples.engagement_reason: He analyzes H^1(O_X) for a characteristic-p curve by decomposing it under Frobenius and explaining how Frobenius powers and finite étale covers kill the two pieces.

passes.reason: ordinary characteristic-p curve example illustrating how Frobenius and finite étale covers annihilate H^1(O_X)
The characteristic-p curve discussion illustrates the positive mechanism for killing H^1(O_X) by covers rather than refuting a claim.
The supersingular elliptic curve specialization has minimal p-rank/Frobenius-nilpotent H^1 and shows finite étale covers alone cannot kill H^1.
The characteristic-p curve setup is standard, but the supersingular/ Frobenius subcase is invoked to show finite étale covers alone cannot suffice.
The characteristic-p curve calculation is used, via the supersingular subcase, to show finite étale covers alone cannot always kill H^1(O_X).
The characteristic-p curve calculation is used to show that finite étale or separable covers alone cannot always kill the Frobenius-nilpotent part of H^1(O_X).
The characteristic-p curve case illustrates the positive mechanism for killing H^1(O_X) by Frobenius plus finite étale covers.
Used to illustrate the characteristic-p mechanism for annihilating H^1(O_X) by passing to Frobenius and finite étale covers.
The characteristic-p curve discussion culminates in the supersingular p-rank-zero case, used to show finite étale covers alone fail and Frobenius is necessary.
The supersingular elliptic-curve subcase is invoked to show finite étale covers alone cannot kill H^1(O_X), and it is extremal by having p-rank zero.
The supersingular/p-rank-zero characteristic-p curve case is invoked to show finite étale covers alone cannot kill H^1(O_X).
The characteristic-p curve calculation illustrates cover-killing, and the supersingular elliptic subcase is used to show finite étale/separable covers alone cannot suffice.

examples.name: Two-variable bad relation ax = by
examples.note: The n=2 case of the local theorem makes the abstract regular-sequence statement concrete by saying that a relation ax=by in R/p becomes a trivial Koszul relation after passing to a finite extension.
examples.engagement_reason: He specializes the local vanishing theorem to the two-variable relation ax=by and explains concretely how passing to a finite extension makes the bad relation trivial.

passes.reason: the relation ax=by is used as a bad relation witnessing failure of regular-sequence divisibility before passing to a finite extension
The relation ax=by is explicitly a bad syzygy obstructing regular-sequence behavior before passing to a finite extension.
The relation ax=by is the minimal two-generator Koszul obstruction measuring failure of regularity before passage to a finite extension.
A nontrivial relation ax=by is a collapse of regular-sequence independence and serves as the bad obstruction class to be killed after finite extension.
The relation ax=by is presented as a bad Koszul relation obstructing regularity before passing to a finite extension.
The relation ax=by is a nontrivial Koszul relation witnessing failure of regularity before passage to a finite extension.
The relation ax=by is presented as the minimal two-variable form of a bad Koszul relation obstructing regularity before passing to a finite extension.
A relation ax=by is explicitly a bad syzygy obstructing regularity until it is killed after finite extension.
The relation ax=by is a minimal bad syzygy obstructing x,y from behaving as a regular sequence.
The relation ax=by is an adversarial non-Koszul relation witnessing the degenerate failure of x,y to be a regular sequence before passing to a cover.
The relation ax=by is a minimal two-variable non-Koszul dependence obstructing regularity before passing to a finite extension.
The relation ax=by is explicitly a bad nontrivial relation obstructing regular-sequence/Cohen--Macaulay behavior before finite extension.

examples.name: Direct summand conjecture for finite extensions of Z[x_1,...,x_n]
examples.note: The finite extension R=Z[x_1,...,x_n] to S is used as an accessible application showing how the Cohen--Macaulayness of R^+/p leads, via flatness, to an R-linear splitting.
examples.engagement_reason: He applies theorem B to the direct summand conjecture and sketches the argument through Cohen–Macaulayness, miracle flatness, freeness after completion, and extracting an R-linear section.

passes.reason: finite extensions of the regular polynomial ring over Z are used as the positive statement/application of the direct summand theorem
